label: La Moneda Palace
sub: Presidential palace bombed by Air Force jets on September 11, 1973. Salvador Allende died here. Site of the coup's defining images: the pink palace in flames.

label: National Stadium
sub: Converted to mass detention center after the coup. Up to 12,000 people held here in September–October 1973. Víctor Jara tortured and killed in the tunnels on September 16, 1973.

label: Villa Grimaldi
sub: DINA's principal torture and detention center, 1974–1978. Over 4,500 people detained; at least 226 killed or disappeared. Now a Memorial Park for Peace in Peñalolén.

label: José Domingo Cañas
sub: DINA detention and torture house in Ñuñoa, Santiago. Used 1973–1974. Several hundred prisoners processed here; documented by the Vicaría de la Solidaridad.

label: Londres 38
sub: First major DINA clandestine detention center in Santiago's city center, used September–November 1973. At least 98 people were disappeared from this address. Now a memorial site.

label: Tres Álamos
sub: Santiago detention camp used from late 1973. Divided into Cuatro Álamos (incommunicado) and Tres Álamos (acknowledged). International observers eventually gained access, unlike earlier secret sites.

label: Peldehue Military Base
sub: Military base north of Santiago used for executions in 1973. Remains of several disappeared detainees were found buried here in subsequent decades.

label: Tejas Verdes (San Antonio)
sub: Army Engineer School camp near San Antonio used as early DINA torture school and detention center. Key site where many early torture methods were developed and systematized under Colonel Manuel Contreras.

label: Colonia Dignidad / Villa Baviera
sub: Secretive German colony near Parral used by DINA as remote torture and execution site. Founded by Nazi fugitive Paul Schäfer. At least 100 political prisoners executed and buried here 1973–1980.

label: Pisagua Detention Camp
sub: Remote nitrate-era port converted to a detention camp after the coup. Prisoners transported to this isolated Atacama coast location. Mass graves discovered here in 1990, including victims of the Caravan of Death.

label: Chacabuco Concentration Camp
sub: Abandoned nitrate company town in the Atacama Desert converted to a concentration camp holding up to 1,400 political prisoners from October 1973. Used until May 1974. One of the most isolated detention sites.

label: Dawson Island Prison Camp
sub: Military prison on an isolated island in the Strait of Magellan. Held approximately 100 senior Allende government officials from September 1973 through 1974 in extreme cold and forced labor conditions.

label: Lonquén Mass Grave
sub: First confirmed mass grave of the regime, discovered November 1978 at an abandoned lime kiln. Remains of 15 peasants arrested in October 1973 identified here. Discovery forced public acknowledgment of disappearances.

label: Patio 29 — General Cemetery
sub: Section of Santiago's General Cemetery where coup victims were buried secretly as NN (no name). Over 100 bodies from September–October 1973 were found here in the 1990s; misidentifications later caused additional trauma for families.

label: Calama — Caravan of Death Site
sub: Northern mining city where 26 prisoners were executed by General Arellano Stark's Caravan of Death in October 1973. Bodies were buried in the Atacama Desert; remains of some victims found at Quebrada de Pisagua in 1990.

label: La Serena — Caravan of Death
sub: Caravan of Death executed 15 prisoners here in October 1973, including local Popular Unity leaders already serving sentences imposed by military courts. The executions exceeded even military tribunal sentences.

label: Copiapó — Caravan of Death
sub: Caravan of Death executed prisoners in Copiapó in October 1973, including regional Popular Unity officials. Remains were buried in the Atacama Desert and some were recovered decades later through forensic investigation.

label: Congreso Nacional (Pre-1973)
sub: Santiago's old Congress building (now used by courts) — seat of the Congress dissolved by Pinochet's Decree Law No. 1 on September 11, 1973. Congress did not reconvene until 1990 in Valparaíso.

label: Valparaíso Naval Base
sub: Chilean Navy seized Valparaíso at 6 AM on September 11, 1973, marking the operational start of the coup. The Navy also tortured suspected leftists on prison ships in the harbor in the weeks following.

label: Military School (Escuela Militar)
sub: Chilean Military School served as coup command post on September 11 and subsequently as a temporary detention center. The Air War Academy near Santiago was also used as a detention facility in September 1973.

label: Chile Stadium (Former Víctor Jara)
sub: Smaller sports venue near the National Stadium also used as a detention center in September 1973. Renamed Estadio Víctor Jara in 2003 to honor the musician tortured and killed at the National Stadium.

label: Recinto de detención CNI Maule
sub: CNI detention sites in the Maule region, south of Santiago, used in conjunction with Colonia Dignidad as a remote area for interrogation and execution of political prisoners during the 1970s.

label: Viña del Mar — Allende's Secret Grave (1973–1990)
sub: Allende was secretly buried here after the coup, his grave unmarked for 17 years. His remains were exhumed in September 1990 and given a state funeral in Santiago.

label: Antofagasta Detention Sites
sub: Military detention facilities in Antofagasta used after the coup. The Caravan of Death executed prisoners here in October 1973. The city's proximity to the Atacama Desert facilitated clandestine burials.

label: Temuco — Southern Resistance
sub: Mapuche indigenous and leftist resistance to the dictatorship was centered in and around Temuco. The city saw significant union and peasant organizing during the Allende years and subsequent severe repression of Mapuche land activists.

label: Concepción — Cordón Industrial
sub: Chile's second-largest city was a major center of the workers' cordones industriales (industrial belts) organized during the October 1972 strike and coup. Strong Popular Unity support; severe post-coup repression.

label: Vicaría de la Solidaridad Offices
sub: The Archdiocese of Santiago provided offices for the Vicaría de la Solidaridad, which operated as the primary human rights documentation and legal defense organization throughout the dictatorship (1976–1992).

label: Iquique — Concentration Camps
sub: Iquique and surrounding nitrate office ghost towns used as detention camps after the coup. The region's geography (extreme desert isolation) made it ideal for hiding prisoners. Caravan of Death passed through in October 1973.

label: Río Mapocho — Bodies Recovered
sub: In the days following the coup, dozens of bodies of executed prisoners were found floating in the Río Mapocho in Santiago. The river became a symbol of the coup's immediate mass killings.

label: Punta Arenas — Military Detention
sub: Chile's southernmost major city was used as a detention hub for prisoners being transferred to Dawson Island. Regional military installations held political prisoners; the Navy was particularly active in Magallanes region repression.

label: Valparaíso Congress Building
sub: New congressional building in Valparaíso, where Chile's democratic Congress was inaugurated in March 1990 after 17 years of military rule. Patricio Aylwin was sworn in here. Congress remained here after restoration of democracy.

label: Universidad Técnica del Estado
sub: Technical university where Víctor Jara and hundreds of students and faculty were arrested on September 12, 1973, the day after the coup. Campus was occupied and searched by military forces. Many detainees taken to the National Stadium.

label: Cuatro Álamos (Secret Section of Tres Álamos)
sub: The secret wing of Tres Álamos detention center where prisoners were held incommunicado for interrogation before being transferred to the officially acknowledged section. Families were not notified of detention here.

label: Clandestine Graves — Colina
sub: Area north of Santiago where remains of coup-era victims were discovered in subsequent decades. Part of ongoing forensic investigation into the fate of the disappeared.

label: Cordón Cerrillos-Maipú Industrial Belt
sub: Workers' industrial belt in southern Santiago that organized worker self-management during the 1972 truckers' strike. One of the strongest centers of Popular Unity working-class support; workers occupied factories to keep production running.

label: FPMR Urban Operations (Santiago)
sub: The Manuel Rodríguez Patriotic Front (FPMR), armed wing of the Communist Party, conducted urban resistance operations in Santiago including the 1986 assassination attempt on Pinochet. The Front operated clandestine networks across the capital.

label: La Victoria — Protest Poblacion
sub: Working-class neighborhood in southern Santiago known for resistance to the dictatorship. Community barricaded streets during national protest days in 1983-1986. Father André Jarlan, a French priest, was killed by police fire during the 1984 protests here.

label: Santiago — Plebiscite Rally Sites
sub: Major plazas and parks in central Santiago where the Concertación coalition held mass rallies for the 'No' campaign in 1988. The October 5, 1988 plebiscite victory was celebrated here by hundreds of thousands.

label: Cauquenes — Caravan of Death
sub: Caravan of Death executed prisoners here in October 1973 who were already in military custody. The operation was conducted by General Arellano Stark's team traveling by helicopter.

label: Chuquicamata Copper Mine
sub: World's largest open-pit copper mine, nationalized under Allende in 1971. The copper workers' confederation (CTC) led the first national protest day against Pinochet in May 1983. The mine remained state-owned through and after the transition.

label: Maipo Canyon — Pinochet Ambush (1986)
sub: Location of the FPMR ambush on Pinochet's motorcade on September 7, 1986. RPG rockets and automatic weapons fire killed five bodyguards; Pinochet escaped with minor wounds. The failed assassination attempt led to a state of siege.

label: Arica — Northern Border Detentions
sub: Extreme northern border city where Chilean political prisoners were transferred to and from Peru and Bolivia as part of Operation Condor cross-border operations. Detention facilities held suspects from the Bolivian and Peruvian leftist movements.
